The **Gold Coast Titans** are a professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football club, based on the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), Australia. The club competes in the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") (NRL) premiership, Australia's elite rugby league competition. Since [2008](/wiki/2008_NRL_season "2008 NRL season"), the Titans have played their home games at [Robina Stadium](/wiki/Robina_Stadium "Robina Stadium") in [Robina](/wiki/Robina,_Queensland "Robina, Queensland"). The Titans joined the NRL in [2007](/wiki/2007_NRL_season "2007 NRL season").

The Gold Coast Titans were the first national-level franchise from the Gold Coast region since the [previous franchise, the Gold Coast-Tweed Giants / Gold Coast Seagulls / Gold Coast Chargers](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Chargers "Gold Coast Chargers"), which existed from [1988](/wiki/1988_NSWRL_season "1988 NSWRL season") to [1998](/wiki/1998_NRL_season "1998 NRL season").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [Gold Coast Titans History](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans_History "Gold Coast Titans History")

See also: [Gold Coast Chargers](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Chargers "Gold Coast Chargers")

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

The beginnings of a Gold Coast team's bid to return to the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") began when the [Australian Rugby League](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League "Australian Rugby League") decided to remove the financially successful [Gold Coast Chargers](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Chargers "Gold Coast Chargers") from the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") at the end of the [1998 Season](/wiki/National_Rugby_League_season_1998 "National Rugby League season 1998"). The ARL wanted to have a second major team based in Brisbane and believed the best way to achieve this was by removing the Gold Coast team from the competition. The attempt to launch a second Brisbane team failed and in 1999, [Michael Searle](/wiki/Michael_Searle_(rugby_league) "Michael Searle (rugby league)"), former Gold Coast Chargers player and Managing Director of International Sports Australia, formed a Gold Coast Bid Team. The team included former Chargers boss [Paul Broughton](/wiki/Paul_Broughton "Paul Broughton") and was labelled as "The Gold Coast Consortium" by the media. The board was constantly active in lobbying the NRL to both expand the competition and consider the Gold Coast's bid for inclusion. It was successful in organising pre-season NRL trial matches to be hosted on the Gold Coast and with average attendances of over 16,000, and peak attendances topping 20,000, the popularity of rugby league on the Coast was clear.

> I firmly believe rugby league should be looking to expand. The Gold Coast area is growing at a phenomenal rate. There are 89 rugby league teams in Gold Coast primary schools and 59 teams in Gold Coast high schools representing about 2,300 young players. That's just schoolboys.
>
> If the catchment area includes the region from South Brisbane to Byron Bay in northern NSW, then it covers a rugby league heartland starving for recognition. These areas would only blossom even more if the young players had a senior team in the NRL competition to which they could aspire.
>
> South-east Queensland needs another NRL team desperately and for a whole host of reasons.

— [Phil Gould](/wiki/Phil_Gould_(rugby_league) "Phil Gould (rugby league)"), 21 December 2003[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In August 2004, the NRL rejected Michael Searle's bid for re-adding a Gold Coast team.[[3]](#cite_note-3) However, later, during an episode of [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network")'s *[The NRL Footy Show](/wiki/The_NRL_Footy_Show "The NRL Footy Show")*, the "Gold Coast Consortium" announced their team name and jersey to the public from the [Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre"). Initially the team was to be named the **Gold Coast Dolphins**, with the team colours to be white, jade and orange. While the Dolphins moniker was popular among many, the heavyweight Brisbane [Queensland Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup") side the [Redcliffe Dolphins](/wiki/Redcliffe_Dolphins "Redcliffe Dolphins") saw it as a threat for any prospect of entering the NRL they had, and as a result threatened severe legal action if the Gold Coast used the Dolphins name. Midway through that year the National Rugby League announced that after viewing submissions from the Gold Coast Dolphins, the [Central Coast Bears](/wiki/Bring_back_the_bears "Bring back the bears") and the [Wellington Orcas](/wiki/Wellington_Orcas "Wellington Orcas"), there would not be a 16th team included in the [2006 NRL competition](/wiki/NRL_season_2006 "NRL season 2006"). Reasons given to the Gold Coast was the National Rugby Leagues concern over the quality and capacity of their home ground, [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium"), which is an oval and only seats 16,000 (although it has been known to fit in 23,000 after minor redevelopment).

Although their bid was knocked back, the consortium continued to push heavily in 2005 for a Gold Coast team, changing the entry year to 2007. The Gold Coast received a massive boost when the Queensland Government announced they would spend A$100 million on a new, 25,000-seat rectangular stadium in [Robina](/wiki/Robina,_Queensland "Robina, Queensland") for the Gold Coast should they be accepted into the 2007 competition. The stadium would not be completed until early 2008 yet the promise of a new first-class facility was enough for the NRL to accept the Gold Coasts bid and on 27 May 2005, NRL CEO [David Gallop](/wiki/David_Gallop "David Gallop") announced that the Gold Coast franchise would be the 16th team in the [2007 NRL season](/wiki/2007_NRL_season "2007 NRL season").

In the time since the Gold Coast was admitted, the Titans completed a vigorous recruitment drive, they announced the signing of [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") assistant coach [John Cartwright](/wiki/John_Cartwright_(rugby_league) "John Cartwright (rugby league)") as the Titans first head-coach and successfully signed contracts with established players such as [Dally M Medal](/wiki/Dally_M_Medal "Dally M Medal") winner [Preston Campbell](/wiki/Preston_Campbell "Preston Campbell"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland_state_rugby_league_team "Queensland state rugby league team") & [Australian](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") representative [Scott Prince](/wiki/Scott_Prince "Scott Prince"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales_state_rugby_league_team "New South Wales state rugby league team") & [Australian](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") representative [Luke Bailey](/wiki/Luke_Bailey_(rugby_league) "Luke Bailey (rugby league)") and enticing former league player [Mat Rogers](/wiki/Mat_Rogers "Mat Rogers") back from rugby union.

### 2007–2008: Laying the foundations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2007–2008: Laying the foundations")]

Main article: [2007 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2007_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2007 Gold Coast Titans season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Performance_Chart_NRL_GCT.svg/250px-Performance_Chart_NRL_GCT.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Performance_Chart_NRL_GCT.svg)

Chart of yearly table positions for Gold Coast Titans in First Grade [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL")

In February 2007, the Titans played their historic first game in a trial against the previous season's Minor-Premiers and Grand Finalists [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), previously the newest club in the League (apart from the merged teams). They were beaten in the club's first hit-out but over the following weeks, Cartwright fielded his full-strength team in two other pre-season trials, winning 22–10 over the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") for the club's first victory. The following week in the team first played in front of their home fans on the Gold Coast, they ran out 28–6 winners over the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers"). Although merely trial matches, the signs were positive for the Titans' debut season to come.

The Titans played their first official match in round 1 of the [2007 NRL season](/wiki/National_Rugby_League_season_2007 "National Rugby League season 2007") against the [St. George Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons"). Their first game was moved from their home ground, [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium"), to [Lang Park](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") in Brisbane due to the huge demand of fans eager to see the new Gold Coast outfit in action, and on 18 March 2007, in front of over 42,000 people they made their debut. Gold Coast performed admirably but after falling 14 points behind in the second half 20–6 they could not manage a full comeback losing a tight match 20–18. Former Queensland State of Origin winger [Chris Walker](/wiki/Chris_Walker_(rugby_league) "Chris Walker (rugby league)"), who had been released by [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") due to off-field troubles in 2006, started his rugby league comeback scoring two tries and two conversions for a 14-point haul for the Titans.

The close loss to St. George Illawarra would not last long in the memories of Titans fans as a week later the Gold Coast franchise recorded their first official victory in another tight match, winning 18–16 against the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") at home. The start of the season saw the Titans only manage four wins in the first ten rounds but a mid-season five-week-long winning streak of four wins and a bye saw the Titans sitting in the top four at the conclusion of round 15. The streak was ended in a poor 22–6 loss against the [Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") at home, then they slumped to a five-match losing streak which included a [golden point](/wiki/Golden_point "Golden point") loss to the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") in Round 17 and a 56–10 loss at the hands of the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") in Round 18. The Gold Coast would not register another win until round 21, but by then they had already slipped to 11th position and needed to obtain maximum points from the final four rounds to confirm a spot in the finals in their maiden season. The side only managed one win from the final four matches, however, missing out on the finals. In the final round, a 50–6 thrashing at the hands of [Melbourne Storm] ended the Titans' debut season on somewhat of a sour note, with the young Gold Coast franchise finishing the season in 12th position with 10 wins, 14 losses and a bye for 22 competition points.

Despite having an outdated home ground as they waited for their new stadium to be completed, the Titans had the second-highest average attendance of all NRL clubs for 2007.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

At the end of 2007 [Luke Bailey](/wiki/Luke_Bailey_(rugby_league) "Luke Bailey (rugby league)") and [Anthony Laffranchi](/wiki/Anthony_Laffranchi "Anthony Laffranchi") were named co-winners of the club's inaugural [Paul Broughton](/wiki/Paul_Broughton "Paul Broughton") Medal for best and fairest player of the season.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In their debut season, the Gold Coast club had turned over $16 million.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Main article: [2008 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2008_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2008 Gold Coast Titans season")

The [2008 NRL season](/wiki/2008_NRL_season "2008 NRL season") was the Titans' second in the National Rugby League. A major boost for the Titans was the signing of past [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland_state_rugby_league_team "Queensland state rugby league team") representative [Ashley Harrison](/wiki/Ashley_Harrison "Ashley Harrison") from the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters").

The Titans played their first official match at their new Stadium, [Robina Stadium](/wiki/Robina_Stadium "Robina Stadium"), against the [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") with the final score being 36–18. Rookie [Jordan Atkins](/wiki/Jordan_Atkins "Jordan Atkins") became only the second person in the history of Australian rugby league to score four tries on debut, alongside Canterbury's [Tony Nash](/wiki/Tony_Nash_(rugby_league) "Tony Nash (rugby league)") in 1942. The sellout 26,974-strong crowd assisted the Titans in firmly establishing that they were a force to be reckoned with in 2008.

The club went on to hit top place on the NRL ladder for the first time after round 6. They managed to maintain top spot on the ladder all the way until round 11, when a loss to Wests Tigers saw them forfeit top spot to the Sydney Roosters, who led in points differential. At the conclusion of Round 14, the Titans were equal first on points, fourth on points differential, employing a perfect home record for inspiration. Despite facing a tough home stretch, the Titans placed admirably in their pursuit of an inaugural finals campaign.

One of the Titans' strongest points in 2008 was the form of halfback Scott Prince, who earned selection for State of Origin. However, early in Game 3, his arm was broken, putting him on the sideline for the season. Subsequently, the Titans lost four straight games.

They finished their season at 13th on the NRL ladder.

This year their turnover increased to $22 million and the club posted a $1.5 million profit.

### 2009–2010: First finals appearance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2009–2010: First finals appearance")]

Main article: [2009 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2009_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2009 Gold Coast Titans season")

The Titans secured four new signings for the 2009 season in [Sam Tagataese](/wiki/Sam_Tagataese "Sam Tagataese"), [Matthew White](/wiki/Matthew_White_(rugby_league) "Matthew White (rugby league)"), [Jackson Nicolau](/wiki/Jackson_Nicolau "Jackson Nicolau") and Raiders speedster [William Zillman](/wiki/William_Zillman "William Zillman").

Similar to the Titans previous seasons, they find themselves on top of the competition ladder after six rounds sitting on ten premiership points alongside their rivals the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"). Early victories against the [Canterbury Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury_Bulldogs "Canterbury Bulldogs") and the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") away from home without skipper [Scott Prince](/wiki/Scott_Prince "Scott Prince"), silenced many of their critics who believed they could not win away from the holiday strip or without their veteran halfback.

Many of the Titans NYC side made their debut and turned into regular first-graders, such as [Esi Tonga](/wiki/Esi_Tonga "Esi Tonga"), [David Mead](/wiki/David_Mead_(rugby_league) "David Mead (rugby league)") and [Kevin Gordon](/wiki/Kevin_Gordon_(rugby_league) "Kevin Gordon (rugby league)"). [Kayne Lawton](/wiki/Kayne_Lawton "Kayne Lawton"), [Bodene Thompson](/wiki/Bodene_Thompson "Bodene Thompson") and [Selasi Berdie](/wiki/Selasi_Berdie "Selasi Berdie") also made their NRL debut.

The club's most successful season so far saw them finish in third position, which was in 2009. They were two points behind the competition front-runners [St. George Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons") and the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs"). In the 2009 finals series, they lost their qualifying final 40–32 against the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), then lost the semi-final 27–2 against the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels").

Main article: [2010 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2010_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2010 Gold Coast Titans season")

The Titans signed five new players for the 2010 season in [Joe Tomane](/wiki/Joe_Tomane "Joe Tomane"), [Marshall Chalk](/wiki/Marshall_Chalk "Marshall Chalk"), [Riley Brown](/wiki/Riley_Brown "Riley Brown"), [Clinton Toopi](/wiki/Clinton_Toopi "Clinton Toopi"), [Greg Bird](/wiki/Greg_Bird_(rugby_league) "Greg Bird (rugby league)") and [Steve Michaels](/wiki/Steve_Michaels "Steve Michaels")

The Titans lost a number of players for the 2010 season, including [Ben Jeffery](/wiki/Ben_Jeffery "Ben Jeffery"), [Brenton Bowen](/wiki/Brenton_Bowen "Brenton Bowen"), [Chris Walker](/wiki/Chris_Walker_(rugby_league) "Chris Walker (rugby league)"), [Daniel Conn](/wiki/Daniel_Conn "Daniel Conn"), [Brett Delaney](/wiki/Brett_Delaney "Brett Delaney") and [Siosaia Vave](/wiki/Siosaia_Vave "Siosaia Vave").

For the second year in succession the Titans qualified for the finals, finishing 4th and thereby earning a home qualifying final against the fifth-placed [Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors"). The Titans' 28–16 win, coupled with losses for two other top four teams, meant that the Titans earned the week off and a right to host a preliminary final at the larger [Lang Park](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park"), which turned out to be its 100th ever premiership match. The Titans lost to the resurgent [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"), 32–6, ending their season one week short of the Grand Final. After 2010 the Titans finished up 4th overall in the [2010 NRL season](/wiki/2010_NRL_season "2010 NRL season"), with [Mat Rogers](/wiki/Mat_Rogers "Mat Rogers") retiring at the conclusion of the season.

### 2011–2012: The wooden spoon and player turnover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2011–2012: The wooden spoon and player turnover")]

Main article: [2011 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2011_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2011 Gold Coast Titans season")

During the off-season the Titans lost inspirational five-eighth [Mat Rogers](/wiki/Mat_Rogers "Mat Rogers") to retirement.

To date the Titans have recorded their worst-ever start to a season, losing four of their first five matches against [St. George Illawarra](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") and [North Queensland](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") by scorelines of 16–25, 16–38, 10–12 and 16–20, respectively. They never recovered from their horror start to the year only winning 6 games all year which is their worst season to date finishing on 16 points and receiving the wooden spoon.

Main article: [2012 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2012_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2012 Gold Coast Titans season")

The Titans signed seven new players for the 2012 season including former [Australian](/wiki/Australian_rugby_league_team "Australian rugby league team") and [NSW](/wiki/New_South_Wales_state_rugby_league_team "New South Wales state rugby league team") representative [Jamal Idris](/wiki/Jamal_Idris "Jamal Idris") and current [QLD](/wiki/Queensland_state_rugby_league_team "Queensland state rugby league team") representative [Nate Myles](/wiki/Nate_Myles "Nate Myles") former Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks prop [Luke Douglas](/wiki/Luke_Douglas "Luke Douglas") They also lost key foundation players such as [Preston Campbell](/wiki/Preston_Campbell "Preston Campbell"), [Anthony Laffranchi](/wiki/Anthony_Laffranchi "Anthony Laffranchi"), [Nathan Friend](/wiki/Nathan_Friend "Nathan Friend") and [Mat Rogers](/wiki/Mat_Rogers "Mat Rogers").

The club started their 2012 with an impressive 18–0 win over the [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") away from home. However, the success was very short-lived, losing the next five in a row, four of which were at home. round 7 saw [Aidan Sezer](/wiki/Aidan_Sezer "Aidan Sezer") make his NRL debut against the [Manly Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly_Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly Warringah Sea Eagles") at [Brookvale Oval](/wiki/Brookvale_Oval "Brookvale Oval"). The Titans produced a 26–14 upset, with Sezer scoring in his debut. Debutant for the Titans Jamal Idris coughing up two tries in the round 7 match-up. The next two games were losses.

From rounds 10–21, the Gold Coast side managed a mid season revival. In the period, the club only lost three times, with seven wins and two byes. The revival was not enough to push them into the finals, as they lost four of their last five matches.

#### Financial problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Financial problems")]

In March 2012 it was found that the Gold Coast had fallen into $35 million debt. The team fought wind-up proceedings in the Federal Court and managed to survive the crippling situation. Newly appointed CEO David May revealed that the club was debt free in 2013.

### 2013–2014: Promising starts, mid-season fadeouts and salary cap breach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2013–2014: Promising starts, mid-season fadeouts and salary cap breach")]

Main article: [2013 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2013_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2013 Gold Coast Titans season")

The Titans only signed a handful of players for the 2013 season, most notably Australian and Queensland representative [Dave Taylor](/wiki/David_Taylor_(rugby_league) "David Taylor (rugby league)"), as well as controversial halfback [Albert Kelly](/wiki/Albert_Kelly "Albert Kelly"), who had been sacked from his previous 2 clubs. The club, however, also lost their captain [Scott Prince](/wiki/Scott_Prince "Scott Prince") to the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos").

The club managed to win 4 of their first 6 matches to put themselves in the top 8. They then lost consecutive matches against the [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") and the [Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors"). Since then, the club and won another 4 from their next 6, to put them at 5th on the ladder. An injury to form centre [Jamal Idris](/wiki/Jamal_Idris "Jamal Idris") for the rest of the season derailed their promising campaign. The club their next 4 matches, seeing them slide outside the top 8. Prop [Ryan James](/wiki/Ryan_James_(rugby_league) "Ryan James (rugby league)") hit a purple patch of form in the last 6 rounds. However, the club only managed to win 3 of these 6 games, which was not enough to push them inside the top 8. The Titans finished on 26 points, 2 points behind the 8th placed [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys")

During the season, the Gold Coast Titans managed to break their widest-ever winning margin (heading into the season) three times. Their biggest-ever win before 2013 was by 26 points against the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") back in 2007. In 2013, the club managed a 36-point win over the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders"), a 38-point win over the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels"), and a 28-point win over the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"). Another positive to come out of the season was the new halves pairing of [Albert Kelly](/wiki/Albert_Kelly "Albert Kelly") and [Aiden Sezer](/wiki/Aiden_Sezer "Aiden Sezer"), who played the majority of the season alongside one another.

Main article: [2014 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2014_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2014 Gold Coast Titans season")

Before the 2014 season started, the Gold Coast Titans lost marquee centre [Jamal Idris](/wiki/Jamal_Idris "Jamal Idris") to the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers"). The centre left the club only 2 years into his 5-year contract, because he wanted to be closer to his family in Sydney. The Titans did not sign any marquee players for the 2014 season, but did pick up Penrith centre [Brad Tighe](/wiki/Brad_Tighe "Brad Tighe"), Melbourne utility [Maurice Blair](/wiki/Maurice_Blair "Maurice Blair"), and Bulldogs forward [Paul Carter](/wiki/Paul_Carter_(rugby_league) "Paul Carter (rugby league)"). One of the Titan's foundation players, [Luke O'Dwyer](/wiki/Luke_O%27Dwyer "Luke O'Dwyer"), also retired.

The beginning of the 2014 season look promising for the Titans. The club managed to win 5 of their first 6 games, putting them at outright 1st on the NRL ladder. This period included a 4-game winning streak, including a nail biting Queensland derby against the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), and a controversial 2-point win over the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") in Melbourne. In rounds 7 and 8, the club recorded a 2-point loss to the Panthers, and a 14-point win over the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") in Sydney. However, the promising start to the year ended there. The club then suffered 6 straight losses, 5 of which were at their home ground. Round 17 saw the Titans record their first win in over two months, with an ugly 4-point win over the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs"). However, the club then suffered a defeat by the out of form [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") on the Gold Coast. In round 19, the Titans travelled to Newcastle for the cornerstone match in the 'Rise for Alex Round'. The Titans managed to produce a 22–6 upset win. Since then, however, the club has lost another 6 straight games, including a dismal 42–0 loss to the [Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors"). This would be the first time the club has been held to nil since their inception in 2007.

In round 24, prop [Luke Douglas](/wiki/Luke_Douglas "Luke Douglas") would miss his first-ever game since his debut in 2006, after being suspended as a result of the [2011 Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks supplements scandal](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks_supplements_saga "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks supplements saga"). His record streak was halted at 215 consecutive games.

From round 11 to round 20, [David Taylor](/wiki/David_Taylor_(rugby_league) "David Taylor (rugby league)") produced his best patch of form since joining the club. This included 5 tries in 6 games, and also earned him a recall into the Queensland State of Origin team for games 2 and 3 of the 2014 series. Halfback [Albert Kelly](/wiki/Albert_Kelly "Albert Kelly") also had a promising start to the season, scoring 5 tries in the opening 5 games, 4 of which were intercept tries. Foundation coach [John Cartwright](/wiki/John_Cartwright_(rugby_league) "John Cartwright (rugby league)") would coach his last game in round 22, after announcing he would be standing down from the role. Assistant coach [Neil Henry](/wiki/Neil_Henry "Neil Henry") will coach the club for the remaining 4 rounds.

The Titans were able to end the difficult year on a high upsetting the Bulldogs who went on the play in the grand final at home 19–18.

After an investigation by the NRL into the Titans and five other clubs (Brisbane Broncos, Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles, Newcastle Knights and Sydney Roosters), the Titans were fined $300,000.

### 2015–2019: NRL takeover; Return to the Finals and disastrous years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2015–2019: NRL takeover; Return to the Finals and disastrous years")]

Main article: [2015 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2015_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2015 Gold Coast Titans season")

On 24 February, the club was placed into [voluntary administration](/wiki/Administration_(law)#Australia "Administration (law)") and its [licence](/wiki/Licence "Licence") was transferred to the [NRL](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") in February 2015.

One day before the 2015 NRL season kicked off, [Daly Cherry-Evans](/wiki/Daly_Cherry-Evans "Daly Cherry-Evans") announced he would join the club on a four-year deal starting from 2016, with the deal reported to be worth over $1 million a season. Titans [Chief Executive Officer](/wiki/Chief_Executive_Officer "Chief Executive Officer"), [Graham Annesley](/wiki/Graham_Annesley "Graham Annesley"), stated "I think this signing sends a positive sign to the rugby league world that this club does have a future and it can attract quality players and it can turn itself around."[[7]](#cite_note-7) However, on 3 June 2015, Cherry-Evans reneged his deal with the Titans, and re-signed with the Manly Warringah Sea Eagles.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

After the money from Cherry-Evans' contract was freed up, due to him no longer joining the club for the 2016 season and beyond, the Titans signed a handful of players during the off-season. Signings included halves [Tyrone Roberts](/wiki/Tyrone_Roberts "Tyrone Roberts") and [Ashley Taylor](/wiki/Ashley_Taylor_(rugby_league) "Ashley Taylor (rugby league)") from the Newcastle Knights and Brisbane Broncos, respectively, former Titans hooker [Nathan Friend](/wiki/Nathan_Friend "Nathan Friend") from the Warriors, props [David Shillington](/wiki/David_Shillington "David Shillington") and [Zeb Taia](/wiki/Zeb_Taia "Zeb Taia") from the Canberra Raiders and Catalans Dragons, and premiership winning second-rower [Chris McQueen](/wiki/Chris_McQueen "Chris McQueen") from the South Sydney Rabbitohs.

Main article: [2016 Gold Coast Titans season](/wiki/2016_Gold_Coast_Titans_season "2016 Gold Coast Titans season")

The first half of the Titans' season was shaky, having won six games, and also losing six games, sitting in the Top 8 by the end of Round 13. During this time, the Gold Coast added Warriors centre [Konrad Hurrell](/wiki/Konrad_Hurrell "Konrad Hurrell") to their roster, as well as former Parramatta hooker [Nathan Peats](/wiki/Nathan_Peats "Nathan Peats"), who was forced out of his former club due to the [club's ongoing salary cap scandal](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels_salary_cap_breach "Parramatta Eels salary cap breach"). On 3 August, the club ultimately made up for the reneging of Daly Cherry Evans by signing two-time Dally M medal winner and cross code superstar [Jarryd Hayne](/wiki/Jarryd_Hayne "Jarryd Hayne"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) who was available to play for the club immediately and was signed until 2017. By Round 26's end, the Gold Coast won 11 games, drew 1 and lost 12, earning 27 premiership points in total. This allowed the club to return to the NRL Finals for the first time since 2010, after the 9th-placed Wests Tigers lost 52–10 to the Canberra Raiders.
The men from the glitter strip played the Brisbane Broncos in a controversial elimination final on 10 September and were knocked out 44–28, ending their 2016 season.

2017 was a year to forget for Gold Coast as they finished 15th on the table at the end of the season. Star recruit [Kevin Proctor](/wiki/Kevin_Proctor "Kevin Proctor") was stripped of his co-captaincy responsibilities in May after allegations relating to drug use while in camp with the Kiwis and Hayne's ankle injury and shifting between fullback and centre became a distraction more than a solution. There was also the ongoing feud between coach Neil Henry and Hayne which resulted in Henry's tenure coming to an end after their Round 24 loss to Parramatta.[[10]](#cite_note-10) To make matters worse, the Titans suffered their heaviest loss to date, losing to the Broncos 54–0 at Robina Stadium.

On 18 October 2017, Gold Coast appointed Garth Brennan as their new coach. At the end of 2017, [Jarryd Hayne](/wiki/Jarryd_Hayne "Jarryd Hayne") left the club to rejoin Parramatta for the 2018 season ending one of the most turbulent times in the club's history.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Before the 2018 NRL season began most experts predicted Gold Coast to be contending for the wooden spoon come season's end but the club shocked many critics after winning three of their first five matches. Gold Coast then went on an inconsistent run of form and ended finishing 14th on the table at the end of the regular season.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Gold Coast started the [2019 NRL season](/wiki/2019_NRL_season "2019 NRL season") badly with the club losing the first four matches before finally recording a victory in round 5 against Penrith. On 14 July 2019, Gold Coast head coach [Garth Brennan](/wiki/Garth_Brennan "Garth Brennan") was terminated by the club following a 24–2 loss against Penrith which left them rooted to the bottom of the table.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
In the aftermath of Brennan's termination, Gold Coast CEO Dennis Watt refuted claims that the struggling club would be relocated to Brisbane saying "For the benefit of Titans Members, fans and sponsors, here are the facts: We are the Gold Coast Titans, and that is how we will stay". Watt later said that the appointment of the next coach would be the club's last stand saying "We're in no doubt that this is it, This is the last stand, we have to get it right, There are plenty of other people banging on the door who would probably like to launch teams elsewhere".[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

On 31 July 2019, Gold Coast announced that [St Helens](/wiki/St_Helens_R.F.C. "St Helens R.F.C.") coach [Justin Holbrook](/wiki/Justin_Holbrook "Justin Holbrook") would be the club's new head coach starting in 2020.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
In Round 20 of the [2019 NRL season](/wiki/2019_NRL_season "2019 NRL season"), the Gold Coast suffered their second heaviest defeat as a club losing 58–6 to the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") at the [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground"). Interim head coach [Craig Hodges](/wiki/Craig_Hodges_(rugby_league) "Craig Hodges (rugby league)") described the loss by saying "The fear is that it is the habit they allow...that becomes acceptable for them individually whether they stay here or whether they move on to other places, And if that's what they take out of this season, if that's their legacy moving forward from this season, I think that's a terrible tragedy".[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

On 5 August 2019, Gold Coast co-captain [Kevin Proctor](/wiki/Kevin_Proctor "Kevin Proctor") wrote an open letter to the club's fans, apologizing for their performance against the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"). Proctor wrote "As a playing group, we were humiliated, embarrassed and appalled at the result and the way that we played as a team, I don't have any explanation for why the game panned out the way it did, It has been a hard year, and you deserve better than the results we have delivered, and we're sorry. We will not give up. We hope that you will not give up on us".[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In Round 22 of the [2019 NRL season](/wiki/2019_NRL_season "2019 NRL season") against the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels"), the Gold Coast lost the match 36–12 at [Robina Stadium](/wiki/Robina_Stadium "Robina Stadium") which all but confirmed that the club would finish last and claim the wooden spoon with just 3 games remaining.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
The Gold Coast were officially handed the wooden spoon the following week as they lost 24–8 against Melbourne at the [Melbourne Rectangular Stadium](/wiki/Melbourne_Rectangular_Stadium "Melbourne Rectangular Stadium"). The wooden spoon capped off a bad year for sport on the Gold Coast as the other team representing the region, the [Gold Coast Suns](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Suns "Gold Coast Suns"), finished last in the [AFL](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### 2020–2025: Holbrook appointed, on-field inconsistency, Hasler appointed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2020–2025: Holbrook appointed, on-field inconsistency, Hasler appointed")]

At the start of the [2020 NRL season](/wiki/2020_NRL_season "2020 NRL season"), the Gold Coast lost their first three matches before defeating the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") 28–23 at [Lang Park](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park"). The victory was the club's first win in 364 days.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The Gold Coast improved significantly in Holbrook's first season, finishing in 9th position, the club's best finish since 2016. The club finished the season with a five game win streak over St. George Illawarra, Canterbury-Bankstown, Brisbane, Manly-Warringah and Newcastle; however, only one of those wins were against a team who made the finals. They also had close losses against [Penrith](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") (who finished 1st) and the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") (who finished 4th).

The club recruited heavily for the [2021 NRL season](/wiki/2021_NRL_season "2021 NRL season"), securing the signings of forwards David Fifita, [Tino Fa'asuamaleaui](/wiki/Tino_Fa%27asuamaleaui "Tino Fa'asuamaleaui") and [Herman Ese'ese](/wiki/Herman_Ese%27ese "Herman Ese'ese").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In the [2021 NRL season](/wiki/2021_NRL_season "2021 NRL season"), the club secured 8th position with a 44–0 victory over [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") on the last day of the regular season, the best in club history. The Gold Coast reached the finals after highly unlikely circumstances after going into the final round in 11th place. The club needed both Cronulla-Sutherland and Canberra to lose their matches and they were also required to win their match over New Zealand by more than 12 points.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In week one of the 2021 Finals Series, the Gold Coast played in the elimination final against the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") where they lost 25–24 ending their season.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
Following the conclusion of the [2021 NRL season](/wiki/2021_NRL_season "2021 NRL season"), Gold Coast CEO Steve Mitchell declared the club would be launching a strategic plan over the next nine years which included the club winning two premierships by 2030 and having sold out crowds for every home game at [Robina Stadium](/wiki/Robina_Stadium "Robina Stadium") with every seat being allocated to a member of the club. The statement was considered bold by sections of the media given the fact that no Gold Coast team had ever reached a grand final before and that the club also had the lowest number of memberships in the [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The Gold Coast started the [2022 NRL season](/wiki/2022_NRL_season "2022 NRL season") looking to build on 2021 and make back to back finals appearances for the first time since 2010. However, the Gold Coast would only win two of their opening four matches and then went on to lose 16 of their next 18 games. By round 20, the Gold Coast sat bottom of the table and were in danger of claiming another [wooden spoon](/wiki/Wooden_spoon_(award) "Wooden spoon (award)"); however, the club would win three of their last four matches to finish 13th on the table.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In round 8 of the [2023 NRL season](/wiki/2023_NRL_season "2023 NRL season"), the Gold Coast side lead newly admitted [Dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins_(NRL) "Dolphins (NRL)") 26–0 but ended up losing the match 28–26 which equalled the biggest comeback in premiership history.[[31]](#cite_note-31) On 22 June 2023, the Gold Coast announced that they had terminated Holbrook's position as head coach and replaced him with [Des Hasler](/wiki/Des_Hasler "Des Hasler") who would start as the new head coach in 2024. The club made the decision despite the side sitting just one place outside the top eight.[[32]](#cite_note-32) After the termination of Holbrook's contact, the club would only go on to win two of their remaining ten matches to finish 14th on the table. The club capped off a difficult season by defeating Canterbury 34–30.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The Gold Coast started the [2024 NRL season](/wiki/2024_NRL_season "2024 NRL season") poorly losing their opening five matches. The Gold Coast were the first team in 22 years and just the third in NRL history to concede 28 points in eight straight games after losing 30–14 to the [Dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins_(NRL) "Dolphins (NRL)") in round 4.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The Gold Coast would go on to lose their opening six matches which was the most losses to start a season since Parramatta in 2018 and South Sydney in 2006. In round 8, the Gold Coast would record their first win of the year upsetting the Warriors 27–24 in Auckland.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
In round 16, the Gold Coast defeated the Warriors 66–6. It was the club's biggest-ever victory and the biggest victory of any Gold Coast side since the first one entered the competition back in 1988. The Gold Coast also recorded the biggest victory by a team running last across the 114-year history of top-flight rugby league in Australia.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The Gold Coast would finish the 2024 season in 14th place on the table.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In the [2025 NRL season](/wiki/2025_NRL_season "2025 NRL season"), the Gold Coast spent the majority of the year at the bottom of the table. Towards the end of the season it was announced that [Des Hasler](/wiki/Des_Hasler "Des Hasler") would be departing the club with [Josh Hannay](/wiki/Josh_Hannay "Josh Hannay") being his replacement in 2026. In the final round of the season, Gold Coast were down 28–10 at half-time against the Wests Tigers knowing that they had to win if they wanted to avoid the wooden spoon. The Gold Coast would go on to win 36–28 and jump ahead of Newcastle due to a better for and against record. Newcastle would lose the following day against Parramatta 66–10 which meant the Gold Coast avoided the wooden spoon. The Gold Coast had some of the worst defensive statistics of the year including that they conceded 24 points or more in 16 matches.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In round 1 of the [2026 NRL season](/wiki/2026_NRL_season "2026 NRL season"), the Gold Coast got off the worst possible start under Hannay's reign losing 50-10 against Cronulla.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Emblem and colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Emblem and colours")]

Although they were accepted into the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League"), they were without a name after dropping "Dolphins" in order to avoid legal action from the [Redcliffe Dolphins](/wiki/Redcliffe_Dolphins "Redcliffe Dolphins"). The consortium aligned themselves with Gold Coast Radio Station [90.9 Sea FM](/wiki/90.9_Sea_FM "90.9 Sea FM"), and set up a competition in which listeners could submit possible names for the new team. This was shortlisted into ten names, which through online voting was shortened to three: Titans, Stingers and Pirates. Online voting continued, with team being branded as the Gold Coast Titans on 21 September 2005. After the team name was chosen, fans were given six jerseys to vote for on the club's website, with option 6 ultimately being successful.

In October 2021, Gold Coast Titans launched a new look to take the young club into the future.The re-designed logo brought in the Titans' core colours that were designed to reflect the Gold Coast home and modernises the iconic emblem.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The contemporary logo shows a steely determination, reflective of the ambitions of the Titans players, coaches and staff. It was the club's first rebrand since admittance into the competition in 2007.

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Stadium")]

For their first season in the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League"), the Titans played out of [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium") on Nerang-Broadbeach Road, while their 27,000-seat stadium in Robina was being completed. Carrara Stadium was cited as one of the main reasons the Gold Coast bid was rejected in their first attempt to rejoin the NRL, due to the fact that Carrara only seated around 16,000, and was an oval.

Work on the new stadium commenced at the end of 2005, and the facility was ready for play before the start of the 2008 NRL season. The project was managed by the same company that constructed [Lang Park](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park"), [the Gabba](/wiki/The_Gabba "The Gabba"), and [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia")—the Sydney 2000 Olympic Stadium. The new stadium has a current (2024) capacity of 27,690—2,690 more than originally planned for.

## Titans TV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Titans TV")]

The Gold Coast Titans operate a TV channel, Titans TV, that serves the Gold Coast area and is carried by Foxtel on the Gold Coast. Its main programming features match highlights and player biographies.

## Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Jetstar_Airbus_A320_supporting_Gold_Coast_Titans_2.jpg/250px-Jetstar_Airbus_A320_supporting_Gold_Coast_Titans_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jetstar_Airbus_A320_supporting_Gold_Coast_Titans_2.jpg)

One of [Jetstars](/wiki/Jetstar "Jetstar")' [Airbus A320s](/wiki/Airbus_A320 "Airbus A320") had Titans badges and large images of several Titans players on both sides of the fuselage

James Frizelle's Automotive Group & Audi Centre Gold Coast, a local car dealership, signed on as the first major sponsorship partner for the franchise, with a deal lasting three years.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

The Gold Coast Titans officially became the "Jetstar Gold Coast Titans", on 20 September 2006, with the announcement of a five-year multimillion-dollar naming rights sponsorship[[58]](#cite_note-58) with [Jetstar](/wiki/Jetstar "Jetstar").

Later in 2006 [Castlemaine Perkins](/wiki/Castlemaine_Perkins "Castlemaine Perkins"), brewer of [XXXX beer](/wiki/Castlemaine_XXXX "Castlemaine XXXX"), signed a five-year sponsorship agreement with the Titans.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Using the *[XXXX Bitter](/wiki/XXXX_Bitter "XXXX Bitter")* brand, Castlemaine Perkins is now a Platinum Sponsor of the Titans.

In 2024, [The Lottery Office](//lotteryoffice.com.au) became the new principal sponsorship partner of the Titans.

## Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Supporters")]

The official Gold Coast Titans [supporter group](/wiki/Supporter_group "Supporter group") is known as "The Legion". The Legion was formed in late 2006 via the Titans online forum. The Legion was founded during the Panthers pre season 2007 trial match with three fans (Brad Newman, Steve Lippis and Dennis Mulheron) in attendance under the Northern goal posts at Carrara Stadium. The trio gained support from other fans and the Legion started to get momentum and numbers. They continued to congregate under the Northern goal posts for several years until the Legion were moved to the Eastern stand at Robina Stadium and now those core group Legion members are known as "The Legion Frontline". Since entering the competition, the Gold Coast have recorded the lowest number of memberships out of all the NRL teams on a yearly basis.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

On 10 March 2020, the Titans officially embraced the name "Legion" for the entire Titans fanbase.[[65]](#cite_note-65) It was decided that "The Legion" was the perfect fit for this purpose—for its symbolic reference to military and gladiatorial strength, as well as a rallying call for Titans supporters to "Join our Legion".

## Feeder clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast_Titans&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Feeder clubs")]

Since joining the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") for Season 2007, the Titans have aligned themselves with three [Queensland Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup") clubs:

* [Tweed Heads Seagulls](/wiki/Tweed_Heads_Seagulls "Tweed Heads Seagulls") (Current)
* [Burleigh Bears](/wiki/Burleigh_Bears "Burleigh Bears") (former)
* [Ipswich Jets](/wiki/Ipswich_Jets "Ipswich Jets") (Current)

In 2009, the Burleigh Bears linked with the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"). However, by the end of the year this arrangement was cancelled, and the Bears re-linked with the Titans.

During preseason before the 2017 [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") season, the Gold Coast Titans announced the new affiliation with the [Central Queensland Capras](/wiki/Central_Queensland_Capras "Central Queensland Capras")

In 2023, the Burleigh Bears aligned themselves with the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") for 2024–26. cancelling this arrangement with Titans, ending their 14-year affiliation.
Later in the year the [Ipswich Jets](/wiki/Ipswich_Jets "Ipswich Jets") announced an affiliation with the Titans for their 2024 season.
